coredata.dc:title: Experimental and numerical study of photocatalysis in Taylor photoreactors with carbon-quantum-dots/BiOCl as catalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Chang
Yan, Wei-Cheng
Ran, Xiong
Pu, Zhong-Wen
Shi, Wei-Dong
coredata.dc:description: A comprehensive CFD model considering the photo radiation, discrete phase dispersion, flow behaviors and photoreactions was built for Taylor‐Couette reactors (TCRs). The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified BiOCl composites which were responsive to visible light were synthesized and used as the catalysts for photodegradation of organic pollutants. The degradation kinetic parameters were obtained experimentally. The validated model was used to investigate the effects of the key parameters of TCRs such as rotation speed, light intensity and catalyst properties. The interactions among the photo radiation field, catalyst distribution, flow field, and degradation efficiency were discussed. The results showed the addition of 8 ωt % CQDs/BiOCl enhanced the degradation efficiency to 97% as compared to pure BiOCl, while the catalyst dosage had a limited impact on degradation performance. This work provides engineering insights in the CQDs-based photocatalysis, and the proposed model can serve as an effective tool for the design and scale-up of the TCRs.

head.ce:title: Experimental and numerical study of photocatalysis in Taylor photoreactors with carbon-quantum-dots/BiOCl as catalysts

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A comprehensive CFD model considering the photo radiation, discrete phase dispersion, flow behaviors and photoreactions was built for Taylor‐Couette reactors (TCRs). The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified BiOCl composites which were responsive to visible light were synthesized and used as the catalysts for photodegradation of organic pollutants. The degradation kinetic parameters were obtained experimentally. The validated model was used to investigate the effects of the key parameters of TCRs such as rotation speed, light intensity and catalyst properties. The interactions among the photo radiation field, catalyst distribution, flow field, and degradation efficiency were discussed. The results showed the addition of 8 ωt % CQDs/BiOCl enhanced the degradation efficiency to 97% as compared to pure BiOCl, while the catalyst dosage had a limited impact on degradation performance. This work provides engineering insights in the CQDs-based photocatalysis, and the proposed model can serve as an effective tool for the design and scale-up of the TCRs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the introduction of the concept of photocatalysis by Fujishima in 1972 (Fujishima and Honda, 1972), the utilization of photocatalysis technology (PCT) has gained significant recognition in the fields of clean energy (Jabeen et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2021) and environmental governance (Mehboob et al., 2022; Shamaila et al., 2017; Yue et al., 2023). This technology, characterized by the affordability and accessibility of semiconductor photocatalysts, as well as the simplicity and convenience of the operating conditions requiring only ambient pressure and temperature, has been a subject of extensive research over several years (Malato et al., 2009). Despite these favorable attributes, the development of PCT obviously relies on the semiconductor photocatalysts and the photocatalytic reactors (PCRs), which are also the two key factors that limit the industrial application of this technology (Ghafoori et al., 2014).

Many researchers are dedicated to the exploration of photocatalysts and the enhancement of their catalytic performance. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) stands as the first-used and most prevalent photocatalyst (Otitoju et al., 2023; Xie et al., 2020), however, being only responsive to ultraviolet (UV) light and having low quantum efficiency. In response to these limitations, a plethora of novel and efficient photocatalysts has emerged in recent years to address these challenges (Li et al., 2016; Noor et al., 2020; Rodgher et al., 2014; Sajjad et al., 2012). Xu et al. used zinc oxide/graphene to produce an efficient photocatalyst which enhanced the degradation of organic dye (Xu et al., 2011). Similarly, Guo et al. synthesized graphene oxide-ferric oxide for the degradation of organic contaminants (Guo et al., 2013). Li et al. utilized cadmium sulfide-cluster-decorated graphene nanosheets to conduct efficient photocatalytic H2 production (Li et al., 2011). Ong et al. fabricated graphene/graphitic carbon nitride nanocomposites to study the high visible-light photoactivity of this material towards carbon dioxide reduction (Ong et al., 2015). Du et al. reviewed that metal organic frameworks were used as photocatalysts to remove antibiotics (Du et al., 2021). In addition to these photocatalysts, bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl) with its unique layered structure also has been extensively studied (Long et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2019). After extensive research efforts, certain photocatalysts have emerged as potential solutions to address the limitations posed by the optical response region of semiconductor materials, expanding their applicability to the visible light range and mitigating the recombination of photogenerated carriers. However, relying solely on material advancements is far from sufficient; these materials are still hindered by issues such as uneven light distribution, insufficient reaction contact time and high operating costs caused by inappropriate reactor design. These challenges will continue to impede the industrial development of PCT.

Designing different types of PCRs based on the synergistic interactions between light, photocatalytic materials, and pollutants has become particularly important. According to the state of photocatalysts, PCRs can be classified into suspended PCRs and immobilized PCRs (Sundar and Kanmani, 2020). Compared to immobilized PCRs, suspended PCRs exhibit superior photon and mass transfer capabilities (Martin et al., 1999). However, traditional reactors cause significant damage to photocatalytic materials, fail to ensure uniform light distribution, and incur high costs. This has prompted researchers to further explore new reactors. Photocatalytic membrane reactors were introduced which can deal with organic pollutants in the wastewater (Kumari et al., 2020). A slurry bubble column reactor was designed for the degradation of typical aromatic compounds with solar radiation (Kamble et al., 2004). A flat-plane photoreactor was designed by Sutisna et al. to dispose wastewater using TiO2 via solar illumination (Rokhmat et al., 2017). Doss et al. invented a foam photoreactor with a small size and flow-through structure to purify the viruses in the air (Doss et al., 2018). Such reactors have their own advantages and solve the problems of the traditional PCT more or less. Nevertheless, they are still unable to meet the needs of uniform light distribution and adequate contact between photocatalysts and pollutants at the same time. Taylor‐Couette reactor (TCR) which gathers the advantages of other reactors mentioned above has been widely used in homogeneous transition metal catalysis, enzymatic reactions and polymer synthesis due to its low shear stress, small axial back-mix, high driving forces of reactions and larger surface contact area for the reaction medium (Schrimpf et al., 2021). Resende et al. immobilized enzymes in agarose-glyoxyl gel beads to perform proteolysis reaction in TCR according to the characteristic of the reactor that it could realize efficient stirring with the completion of the fragile beads (Resende et al., 2005). As for particle synthesis, barium sulfate particles could be synthesized with controllable shear by using TCR (Liu et al., 2020). It has been also proved that the uniform exposure of catalysts to the light in TCRs can be achieved (Dutta and Ray, 2004; Wang et al., 2023). Therefore, the application of TCRs in the field of PCT is especially attractive and beneficial. Researchers have prospectively applied TCRs as the key reactor of PCT and studied the effect of operating parameters on the performance of TCR in terms of the flow state and internal forces of the solution in TCR (Forney and Pierson, 2003; Sczechowski et al., 1995; Subramanian and Kannan, 2010). However, it is still in lack of a comprehensive understanding of the interactions among light, photocatalytic materials and solutions, which continues to impede the development of corresponding reactor technologies. See (Table 1 ).

For better quantifying the light distribution and understanding its interaction with flow field, heat and mass transfer as well as photocatalytic reactions in TCRs, a comprehensive CFD model was built to analyze the photo radiation field, discrete phase distribution, flow field together with species reaction kinetics. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified ultrathin BiOCl being responsive to visible light was synthesized and used as the model catalyst for photodegradation of organic pollutants in TCR. Rhodamine B (RhB) was used as the representative organic pollutant. The degradation reaction kinetic parameters were obtained experimentally by changing the content and composition of photocatalysts. The established model was validated with the experimental data. Finally, the effects of the key parameters of TCRs such as the rotation speed of the inner cylinder and the light intensity were analyzed to shed light on the interaction among the photo radiation field, discrete phase distribution, flow field, and species reaction.

CQDs/BiOCl composite was synthesized through a solvothermal method mentioned in previous works (Di et al., 2015; Guan et al., 2013). Briefly, 0.243 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 0.2 g of PVP and different amounts of CQDs (3, 5 and 8 ωt %) were dissolved/dispersed in a 15 mL mannitol solution (0.1 M). This solution was defined as solution A. Solution B consisting of 0.029 g of NaCl and 5 mL of H2O was added into solution A drop by drop under continuous stirring. After 30 min, a uniform suspension yielded by the mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 3 h. Subsequently, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally, and the resulting solid product washed with deionized water and ethanol several times was collected. The final powders were dried at 50 °C for 12 h.

To confirm the successful recombination of CQDs and BiOCl, different characterizations were performed. Morphologies of the pure BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl composite were characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Apreo S, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The crystal structures and functional bonding of CQDs, pure BiOCl and three kinds of CQDs/BiOCl composites were characterized by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) using the XRD-6100 Lab (Shimadzu, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation, a Raman spectrometer (DXR, Thermo Fisher, USA) and a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, Nicolet, USA). The optical performance of CQDs, pure BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl composites were investigated by a UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (UV-3600 Plus, Shimadzu, Japan).

The photocatalytic activities of the CQDs/BiOCl composite (20 mg) were evaluated via the photodegradation experiment of 100 mL RhB (10 mg/L) in a GHX-3 photochemical reaction apparatus and the main reactions (Dijkstra et al., 2002; Hoffmann et al., 1995) occurred during the photocatalytic process are listed in Tab. S1. A 300 W Xe lamp was used as the light source to provide visible light. The degradation experiments were implemented at 30 °C via a circulating water system to keep the temperature. Before each degradation experiment, the RhB solution containing photocatalysts was put in the dark with constant stirring for 30 min to achieve the equilibrium of adsorption and desorption. It is worth to note that the adsorption has little influence on the whole photodegradation according to the conclusion of the experiments carried out by Peng et al (Peng et al., 2022). 3 mL solution was sampled at the interval of 10 min for measurement. The concentration of RhB in the sample was measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu, Japan) at λ = 553 nm.

The physical model of the TCR used in this study was constructed by referring to the apparatus reported in Dutta et al.’s work (Dutta and Ray, 2004). The schematic illustration of the TCR is shown in Fig. 1 . The reactor is made up of two coaxial cylinders made of Perspex, a Xe lamp and a speed-controlling motor. The motor is connected to the inner cylinder to change the rotation speeds while the outer cylinder keeps stationary. The RhB solution is injected from the inlet on the outer cylinder and the outlet is kept closed until the solution fills the gap. To prevent thermal catalytic effects, cooling water is placed close to the lamp and kept cycling. Tab. S2 shows the dimensions of the TCR and operating conditions. Meanwhile, to analyze the TCR with generalized parameters, three dimensionless quantities were defined as follows:

where Re is Reynolds number, ω (rad/s) is the rotation speed of the TCR and ν (m2/s) (ν = μ/ρl) is the kinematic viscosity of the solution. rin and ro are the radii of the inner and outer cylinders, respectively. H and d are the height of the TCR and the gap size between the two cylinders. The radius ratio η in this study is equal to 0.75 and the aspect ratio Г is equal to 28.3.

The Euler-Euler approach (Gao et al., 2017) was applied to couple the gas phase and liquid phase in the turbulent flow system. According to the Euler-Euler two fluid model (TFM), the mass and momentum conversation equations can be described as:

U, τ, g and F are the velocity, stress, gravitational acceleration and interphase forces, respectively. Herein, F refers to three forces which are drag force, lift force and turbulent dispersion force. See (Table 2 ).

The drag force is described by the model proposed by Tomiyama et al. (Tomiyama et al., 1998) which is commonly used in gas–liquid flows. The lift force is described by the model proposed by Legendre and Magnaudet (Legendre and Magnaudet, 1998) which is suitable for non-distorted liquid bubbles. These two forces can be expressed as:

At the same time, the discrete ordinate (DO) model was used to describe the photo radiation intensity distribution by solving the radiative transfer equation (RTE). In the DO model, both the angular discretization and pixilation were set as 5. The boundary condition type of all the walls was set as semi-transparent. Then, RTE can be simplified as follows (Sobhani and Shokouhmand, 2017):

The particle radiation interaction is also added to simulate the scattering effect caused by particles.

The flow field and the radiation field can be obtained by the models mentioned above. To further study the interaction among the reaction kinetics, flow field and radiation field, the species transport model was included. The coupling of the photoreactions is achieved by the user-defined functions (UDFs).

The numerical simulation was performed with ANSYS Fluent. A three-dimensional calculation domain was constructed by ANSYS ICEM. UDFs were used to couple the photoreaction kinetics with the integrated TFM model, DO model and Euler-Lagrangian model. A Coupled algorithm was employed to address the pressure–velocity coupling issue, and distorted surface gradient correction and high-order term relaxation were applied to assist the calculations, ensuring more accurate results. The boundary conditions can be found in Tab. 1, and the schematic illustration of model coupling strategies can be seen in Fig. 2 .

As shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. S1, the morphologies characterized by TEM and the elemental composition and mapping performed by SEM prove that ultrathin nanosheets BiOCl were synthesized successfully. The high magnification TEM images of 5ωt% CQDs/BiOCl confirms that the lattice spacing of BiOCl is 0.275 nm and the angle between two sets of lattice fringe is 90°, which is in accordance with the basic theory. The XRD results of BiOCl, CQDs/BiOCl composites (JCPDS card: no. 73–2060, Di et al., 2015) are displayed in Fig. 4 a and that of CQDs (JCPDS card: no. 41–1487) is presented in Fig. S2. Fig. 4b shows the Raman spectra of CQDs, BiOCl and 5 ωt% CQDs/BiOCl. There are bands appearing at 60 cm−1, 142 cm -1, 400 cm−1, 1350 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1. The band at 60 cm−1 is ascribed to the A1g external Bi-Cl stretching mode and the band at 400 cm−1 is ascribed to the motion of O atmos. The band at 142 cm -1 is ascribed to the A1g internal Bi-Cl stretching mode and the Eg internal Bi-Cl stretching mode (Cui et al., 2017). The bands at 1350 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 are produced by the motion of C atmos (Jamila et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2020b). The FTIR spectra of CQDs, BiOCl and 5 ωt% CQDs/BiOCl can be seen in Fig. 4c. The peak at around 526 cm−1 corresponds to the A2u-type vibrations of the Bi-O bonds, and the peak in the region of 1200–1700 cm−1 attributes to the C-C vibrations (Cui et al., 2017). Fig. 4d and Fig. S3 display the UV–Vis DRS spectra and Tauc plot of CQDs and BiOCl, respectively. The band gap Eg (Kanwal et al., 2021; Kanwal et al., 2022) of BiOCl is 2.4 eV which is less than that of CQDs (2.5 eV). It can be inferred that as a material with a relatively small direct band gap, BiOCl possesses a broad photo-response range. The addition of CQDs for the modification of BiOCl can promote the separation of photoelectrons from vacancies. The photodegradation kinetics of RhB with different catalysts is shown in Fig. 5 . The concentration of the residual RhB keeps decreasing sharply with the ongoing photocatalytic process and the degradation rate tends to be 0 after 40 min with a degradation efficiency up to 95 %. Although BiOCl exhibits good photocatalytic activity on its own, the introduction of CQDs enhances the performance of BiOCl. This enhancement is attributed to the formation of nanoscale heterojunctions between BiOCl and CQDs, facilitating close contact between the two materials. These heterojunctions can increase the interfacial area of photocatalytic materials, promoting the acceleration of charge transfer processes. It is worth noting that the direct photolysis without the photocatalysts of RhB can be neglected as compared with the corresponding photocatalysis process (Di et al., 2015). With this experimental data, the reaction kinetics can be derived. From Fig. 5(b), it can be observed that the experimentally obtained residual RhB concentrations fall within the confidence interval of the fitted curve. Therefore, it can be considered that this photodegradation process with such catalysts follows the Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism. The corresponding reaction kinetics can be described by (Ohtani, 2011):

where RRhB is the reaction rate, and kr (min−1) is the apparent rate constant obtained from the slopes of the lines shown in Fig. 5(b). The corresponding value can be seen in Tab. 2. The adsorption equilibrium constant K (K = 6.25 m3/mol) obtained from Houas et al.’s work. (Houas et al., 2001) is in accordance with that of pollutants such as methylene blue which are easily soluble in water. Ct (kg/m3) is the concentration of RhB at time t. With the increasing mass of CQDs added for modification of BiOCl, kr becomes larger which further proves the advantages of the CQDs/ BiOCl composites.

The degradation ratio D (%) was defined to measure the residual RhB in water, which can be described by:

Taking the photo radiation and the photocatalysts into consideration, the local reaction rate of RhB (RRhB,l) under the radiation distribution can be expressed as (Liu et al., 2019):

where M is the molar mass of RhB, e (W/m2) is the radiation intensity representing the charge carriers absorbed by the photocatalysts. I (W/m2) is the intensity of the light source. This local reaction rate equation of RhB was then included into the CFD model via UDFs.

Turbulence is essential to the TCR. With different rotation speeds, there are several flow regimes such as laminar Couette-Taylor flow (CTF), Talyor vortex flow, wavy vortex flow and turbulent vortex flow (Andereck et al., 1986) depending on the Taylor number, Ta which is defined by geometrical parameters and the rotation speeds (Desmet et al., 1996):
(22)
Ta =
ω
d
3 / 2
r
in
1 / 2
ν

According to the previous work, it is known that the flow regime gradually changes when Ta varies from 500 to 103. When Ta is over 4 × 104, the flow will lose the feature of the TCR and become a fully turbulent flow due to the disappearance of the straight radial vortex boundary. In this study, the flow patterns with Ta ranging from 103 to 104 was simulated. The flow regimes at different rotation speeds are the states between Taylor vortex flow and turbulent vortex flow. Fig. 6 presents the contours and vectors of liquid flow at different rotation speeds. It can be seen that the fluid velocity near the outer cylinder increases gradually with the rotation speed of the inner cylinder. Each stable radial velocity corresponds to a vortex, and the vortex effectively maintains the spatial periodic structure. The combination of advection and diffusion divides the internal fluid of TCR into numerous layers, each of which generates several equally sized vortices. Advection determines the overall residence time of the fluid, while diffusion dominates the mass transfer process. Under different states at various rotational speeds, different numbers of layers are formed inside the reactor. Although higher speeds result in fewer layers, each vortex is larger than those at lower speeds. A greater number of layers implies a predominant role of advection in the TCR system, leading to a longer contact time between the solution and the photocatalytic material. Larger vortices indicate that more photocatalytic material is entrained by the solution, resulting in enhanced mass transfer processes. Choosing the appropriate flow conditions allows not only for sufficient contact area between the photocatalytic material and the solution but also increases the contact time. It is crucial to select the proper flow state that enables full contact of photocatalysts with the solution according to the industrial or experimental requirements.

With the establishment of the integrated models and the analysis of the hydrodynamic of the TCR, 20 mg of 5 ωt % CQDs/BiOCl was selected in this study to valid the model due to its high R2 value of 99.9 % for the kr parameter. A weak turbulent vortex with a rotational speed of 43 rpm was selected, as it represents the optimal form of vortex flow. The experimental temperature was set to 303 K under the specified conditions. Fig. 7 gives the comparison of the concentration of residual RhB in the solution between the experimental data and the simulation results, which shows a good agreement.

To conduct a generalized analysis of the system, the physical dimensions of the TCR were defined using the following dimensionless scales:

where rx and ry represent the radial position at the x-direction and the y-direction. h is the vertical position from the bottom of the TCR.

Fig. 8 a and 8c depict that the photocatalytic material floats and disperses throughout the reactor following the flow pattern of the solution. Because the velocity of the vortices remains nearly constant as time proceeds, as shown in Fig. 9 a-d, these vortices are highly stable. In contrast to traditional stirred reactors, this distribution would minimize particle exposure to high shear stress, which can avoid the destruction of fragile catalysts. Moreover, the increased contact area and prolonged residence time facilitate the thorough progression of the photocatalytic reactions.

The patterns of photon distribution within the reactor are shown in Fig. 8b and 8d, which indicates that the existence of particles would affect the distribution of radiation intensity. The higher photo radiation region is located at the regions where more photocatalyst materials appear. This could be due to that the photons are blocked by the photocatalyst via scattering or reflection when penetrating the reactor and accumulate at those regions with high concentrations of the photocatalyst. What is more, it can also be found that the overall photo radiation intensity is higher in the regions near the light source. Moreira et al. (Moreira and Puma, 2021) evaluated the Local Volumetric Rate of Photon Absorption (LVRPA). The simulated radiation intensity distribution in the TCR is consistent with the distribution of LVRPA reported in Moreira et al.’s work. Fig. 9e-g demonstrate that the distribution of the photon covers almost the whole reactor and has little changes as time proceeds.Figs.

The operating conditions such as the rotation speeds and the intensity of the light source of the TCR are the key parameters influencing the flow state of the fluid, the reaction temperature as well as the absorption of the photon. Considering the impact of these factors, the rotation speeds of 43, 114 and 200 rpm were selected for simulation to discuss its effects on the photodegradation performance. The source light intensity of 310, 320 and 330 W were selected to explore its influence. 20 mg of 5 ωt % CQDs/BiOCl was used as the condition for the photocatalyst. Several dimensionless numbers were defined prior to the analysis (Schrimpf et al., 2021):
(26)
Re
az
=
u
az
∙ d
ν
(27)
Nu =
α ∙ 2 d
λ l
where Reaz is the axial Reynolds number, uaz (m/s) is the axial velocity of the fluid (z-direction), Nu is the Nusselt number which is commonly used to characterize the heat transfer, and α (W/m2•K) is the heat transfer coefficient from wall to the fluid.

Fig. 10 a reveals that the degradation rates of the RhB with rotation speeds of 114 rpm and 200 rpm are faster than that with 43 rpm at the first 15 min. As illustrated in Fig. 11 a-b, the distribution of photocatalysts is more uniform at higher rotation speeds in comparison to the distribution depicted in Fig. 8b. When the rotation speed is 43 rpm, more photocatalysts tend to settle at the bottom region of the TCR (Fig. 8b), leading to a low utilization rate of the photons at the upper regions. This could be the reason causing the lower degradation rate at lower rotation speed. When the rotational speed is increased to 200 rpm, although the vortex gradually begins to become unstable according to the distribution of particles, the propagation of photons is less hindered by the photocatalysts in TCR. The velocity distributions of the fluid at different rotation speeds can be seen in Fig. 12 a. The Reaz distributions in the TCR (Fig. 12b) demonstrate that with the increase in rotational speed, the axial velocity distribution of the solution exhibits a higher value in the central part of the reactor, while it is lower at the top and bottom. Simultaneously, the axial velocity of the solution decreases as it gets closer to the outer wall of the inner cylinder. This phenomenon is explained by the fact that the solution near the outer wall of the inner cylinder experiences a higher shear force compared to the inner wall of the outer cylinder (Fig. 12c), creating resistance and consequently reducing its velocity. As the rotational speed increases, the average τW at the outer wall of the inner cylinder also increases monotonically. While this contributes to the formation of vortices, it may reduce convective flow, explaining why the degradation efficiency obtained at 200 rpm is slightly lower than that achieved at 114 rpm. It is shown that for 103 < Ta < 104, the value of Nu is less than 15 (Fig. 13 a), indicating that heat conduction dominates within the reactor. A larger Nu value indicates better heat transfer efficiency between the solution and the wall surface (Fénot et al., 2011). The Nu values near the outer wall of the inner cylinder (λ* = 0.75) exhibit fluctuations along the axial direction, while those near the inner wall of the outer cylinder (λ* = 1) show minimal fluctuations, and their magnitudes do not significantly increase with the increase in rotational speed. This may be influenced by the distribution of particles. At 43 rpm, where particles tend to accumulate more at the bottom, the Nu values are lower compared to those at 114 and 200 rpm, especially when Г* < 10 (near the bottom of the reactor). The catalyst is more evenly dispersed in the upper part of the reactor, allowing heat to diffuse correspondingly with vortices. However, due to the interplay between convection and diffusion, the average Nu near the outer wall of the inner cylinder is highest at 114 rpm, indicating the best heat transfer efficiency at that speed.

The radiation intensity is another factor influencing the degradation of RhB. Fig. 10b illustrates the degradation efficiency of RhB using lamps with different powers. As the xenon lamp power increases, the degradation process does not improve. Instead, the catalytic degradation rate at 300 W is higher than that at 310 and 320 W in the first 40 min. From Fig. 8b, 11c-d, it can be observed that the distributions of photons in the TCR remain consistent for different source light powers while higher power gives a higher value of the overall radiation intensity accumulated in the reactors. To explore the reasons for the decrease in degradation efficiency with light power, the distribution of heat in the TCR was analyzed. Although the average Nu value near the outer wall of the inner cylinder (λ* = 0.75) keeps consistent at the three light power levels, the one near the inner wall of the outer cylinder (λ* = 1) decreases with increasing power (Fig. 13b). At λ* = 1, the average Nu value at 300 W is 4.5, almost twice that at 320 W. This indicates that the increase in light power does not improve heat transfer efficiency. With more photons, the poor heat transfer efficiency and high local temperature in the TCR during the reaction may cause catalyst deactivation. As a result, the catalyst at 300 W achieves higher degradation efficiency than at 310 and 320 W.

Therefore, excessively increasing the rotational speed of the inner cylinder and the power of the light source cannot enhance the photocatalytic activity of photocatalytic materials; instead, it may disrupt the inherent properties of particles, leading to deactivation.

The final photocatalytic performance is primarily determined by the properties of the photocatalyst. Other than the catalytic activity, these particles not only affect the distribution of the radiation field but also influence the flow field. Considering these issues, we investigated the effect of catalyst composite with different quantities of CQDs and the mass of catalysts. The proportions of CQDs in the composites were set as 0, 3, 5, and 8 ωt %. 10, 20, and 30 mg catalysts (5 ωt % CQDs/BiOCl) were selected for simulation. A lamp power of 300 W and an inner cylinder rotational speed of 43 rpm were used as the operating conditions.

The varying proportions of CQDs imply changes in the material, necessitating the inclusion of new kinetics parameters which were listed in Tab. 2. Comparing the simulation results with experimental data reveals a good agreement (Fig. 14 a). As shown in Fig. 14a, the degradation efficiency increases with an increasing proportion of CQDs. The addition of 8 ωt % CQDs/BiOCl can enhance the degradation efficiency to nearly 97 %, whereas pure BiOCl achieves only 91 % degradation efficiency. The improvement in the photocatalytic process relies not only on the enhanced photocatalytic activity but also benefits from the interaction of catalysts with flow fields and photo distributions in the TCR. Fig. 15 a-c demonstrate that as the content of CQDs in CQDs/BiOCl increases, photons are more efficiently absorbed by the catalyst, thereby enhancing the performance of RhB degradation. At λ* = 0.75, the average Nu of 8 ωt % CQDs/BiOCl is 0.07 and 0.24 higher than that of 3 ωt % CQDs/BiOCl and pure BiOCl, respectively (Fig. 16 a). With the addition of CQDs, the axial velocity distribution represented by Reaz becomes more periodic (Fig. 17 a). The average values of Reaz for 3, 5 and 8 ωt % CQDs/BiOCl within the reactor reach 43.28, 42.93 and 45.57, respectively, which are higher than that for pure BiOCl. Higher Reaz indicates a high axial velocity of vortices formed in the TCR and better mixing of the photocatalysts with RhB solution. However, the values of Reaz for 3 ωt % CQDs/BiOCl are more compact, implying that the particles are not sufficiently entrained by the solution. This would result in poorer photon absorption, thus giving rise to the lower degradation efficiency of the RhB solution compared.

To explore whether increasing the amount of photocatalyst can enhance the photocatalytic degradation efficiency, we selected 10, 20, and 30 mg of 5 ωt% CQDs/BiOCl to investigate its effects. Fig. 14b indicates that the degradation efficiency of different amounts of catalysts is nearly consistent. However, with an increase in the amount of catalysts, the degradation rate for 30 mg of catalyst is lower than that for 10 and 20 mg during the first 20 min. Yet, after 40 min, the catalyst is fully utilized, and the final degradation efficiency for 30 mg of catalyst is slightly higher than the efficiency in the other two cases. Overall, the catalyst dosage has a limited impact on degradation performance in this case. To ensure the accuracy of the results, further exploration was conducted on the interactions between heat transfer and the interactions among catalysts, radiation field, and flow field. The radiation intensity and particle distribution are depicted in Fig. 15d-e. As more particles are added, more photons are absorbed, explaining the highest final degradation efficiency observed for 30 mg of catalyst. Although the average values of Nu near the inner wall of the outer cylinder for the different amounts of catalyst are around 4.59, the average Nu value near the outer wall of the inner cylinder for 10 mg of catalyst is approximately 0.45 higher than that for 20 and 30 mg (Fig. 16b). This indicates better heat transfer efficiency for 10 mg of catalyst. The values of Reaz for 10 mg of catalyst are more periodic than that for 20 and 30 mg (Fig. 17b). This suggests that the solution with 10 mg of catalyst has uniformly sized vortices. In addition, each vortex formed has a higher axial velocity. This would lead to a more even dispersion of catalyst in the solution and thorough contact of them with the solution for reactions, resulting in a faster degradation rate for 10 mg of catalyst before 20 min. However, due to the inherent limitations in the degradation capability of the catalysts, the final degradation efficiency for low-mass catalysts is slightly lower than that for high-mass catalysts.

In summary, the addition of CQDs does enhance the photocatalytic degradation capability of BiOCl materials. However, merely increasing the mass of CQDs/BiOCl material does not guarantee a definite improvement in degradation efficiency. Therefore, it is necessary to consider the flow field, optical radiation field, distribution of discrete phases, and reaction kinetics at the same time. This combinational consideration is essential to explore reactors suitable for different materials or determine the appropriate catalyst dosage and ratio within the reactor.

Utilizing PCT as an effective method for eliminating water pollutants can be integrated with the TCR, which extends the residual time of the fluid and ensures thorough contact between the photocatalysts and the contaminated solution. However, the reaction process in TCR is like a “black-box”, causing difficulty in exploring the proper conditions of the reactor for the scale-up of the PCT. In this work, a comprehensive CFD model was built to analyze the photo radiation field, discrete phase distribution, and flow field together with species reaction kinetics. The model was validated through the experiment of the photodegradation of the RhB solution with CQDs/BiOCl. The rotation speeds and the source light power were changed to figure out the impacts of the operating conditions of the TCR on the photocatalytic process. The effect of the catalyst properties such as the composition proportion and the mass of the photocatalysts were also investigated. The following conclusions can be drawn:
(1)
The advances of the TCR can be fully utilized based on its characteristics during photocatalysis. When the rotational speed of the inner cylinder matches a certain quantity of particles, the photocatalytic material can disperse within the solution through vortices, efficiently absorbing photons. This promotes mass transfer and the progress of the photocatalytic degradation process through enhanced convection and thorough contact with the solution.
(2)
The degradation efficiency is influenced by the performance of the reactor. The rotational speed of the inner cylinder and the xenon lamp power affect the distribution and performance of particles, altering photon absorption levels. Simultaneously, they also impact the axial velocity of the solution, thereby influencing heat transfer efficiency and the thorough contact between the catalyst and the solution.
(3)
The addition of 8 ωt % CQDs/BiOCl can enhance the degradation efficiency from 91 % to nearly 97 % compared to pure BiOCl. The catalyst dosage has a limited impact on degradation performance.

Comprehensive consideration of the relationships among the flow field, the radiation field and the reaction kinetics can be beneficial to obtain a better degradation efficiency of the RhB solution. This work provides engineering insights in the carbon-quantum-dots based photocatalysis, and the proposed model can serve as an effective tool for the design and scale-up of the TCR for the photocatalysis industry.
